artifacts.content:
Subject: Can we talk tonight (properly)?

Hi Em,

I’m hiding in one of the little booths on 7 at Exchange House between drafts and just needed to get this all out before I head home for bath time.

I spoke to HR again this afternoon about the performance plan Jonathan put me on after that brutal feedback back in October. They were surprisingly frank – basically said that if I decide to resign before the review meeting on 10 April (they’ve already booked a room on 7 for it), they’d probably be willing to waive some of the three‑month notice. In practice that could mean I’m out of here in four to six weeks rather than slogging through the full period, depending on what we do with the seven days of holiday I’ve got banked.

The flip side is my partner keeps hinting they’ll try to hold me to as much of the notice as they can if I do go, because of “team continuity”, so I don’t want to bank on a miracle. And with the ICSID Latin America case still rumbling on, I also don’t want to dump everything on the juniors.

We also need to think about the Nationwide situation. If we’re still two months behind on the mortgage for Curtain Road come the end of March, I really can’t afford any gap in pay. I know you’ve got parents’ evening hell coming up at the school in Hackney, but could we carve out half an hour tonight after Oliver’s asleep to talk through budgets and what an early move would actually look like? I’m conscious I’m catastrophising and I don’t want to just spring a resignation on you.

I’ll grab milk on the way back – text me if we need anything else.

D x

Title: Work / move / hearings – rough timeline

- Contractual notice at HSF is three months. In reality, because of the South American state‑owned entity case, it might be closer to four months before I can leave without shafting the team.

- Michael Reynolds has already said he wants me to stay through the ICC merits hearing in Paris the week of 4 May 2026. Client (Ministry of Energy in Bogotá) keeps stressing “continuity of counsel” in their emails.

- Earliest honourable resignation point: immediately after we’re back from Paris and I’ve done a basic handover memo. If I give notice then and they insist on the full three months, that takes me to early August 2026 as a realistic end date.

- Need to overlay this with personal stuff:
  • Emma’s C‑section at Homerton is booked for 20 June 2026. I’ve promised two consecutive weeks of shared parental leave straight after to help with the new baby and Oliver.
  • CBT at City & Hackney runs on Tuesday evenings until mid‑May 2026 (12‑session block Dr Carter wants me to finish before any big changes).

- Ideal world: finish CBT mid‑May → Paris hearing early May (overlapping but manageable) → baby arrives 20 June → take SPL → resign early July once we know everyone’s okay.

- That would mean a notice period running into early October 2026. In a fantasy scenario I’d then take a couple of weeks completely off before starting anywhere else, just to decompress after the last few years.

- Remember: also have that ICSID file where VF were co‑counsel and the April 2022 tracked‑changes debacle – keep that in mind when thinking about future colleagues there.

Subject: Coffee with Maria & general wobble

Hi Mum,

On the train back from Holborn so thought I’d send a quick update before the evening chaos. Oliver apparently spent the whole afternoon at nursery pretending to be a dinosaur, so brace yourself for roaring when you next visit.

I met up with Maria Fernández near Lincoln’s Inn this afternoon – we hadn’t seen each other properly since LSE days, apart from the odd LinkedIn message. You remember she sort of took over supervising my dissertation in 2016 when Professor Crawford’s cancer came back and I was missing deadlines and not sleeping? We used to have those twice‑weekly chats in her office at the LSE Law Department after I failed that first draft in February. She was very kind about it all today, said she’s noticed a lot of people burning out in big arbitration teams lately.

We ended up talking about my mid‑year review at work and the whole anxiety/insomnia cycle rearing its head again. I told her I’m doing CBT sessions on Tuesday evenings at the City and Hackney Centre for Mental Health – started back in November after that horrible Dubai arbitration – and she said finishing the 12‑session block before making any big decisions sounded sensible.

She also mentioned she’s now Counsel at Volterra Fietta and joked that she’s seen me grow up from the panicky LLM student who lived in Bethnal Green with “too many books and not enough sleep”.

Don’t worry, I’m not about to do anything rash. Between the mortgage stuff, Emma’s classes in Hackney, and Oliver’s nursery fees, I know we need stability. But it was oddly reassuring to talk to someone who remembers the 2016 wobble and doesn’t think I’m a complete disaster.

Love,

Dan x

metadata.subject: Coffee with Maria & general wobble
metadata.location: Elizabeth line, Liverpool Street to Whitechapel

artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick question about notice periods & sanity

Hi Alex,

Hope chambers is treating you kindly and that you’re getting at least the occasional full weekend off. We’re fine – Oliver has discovered the joy of muddy puddles in the park off Columbia Road, so my Saturdays now involve a lot of laundry.

I wanted to pick your brain about something work‑related. I’m toying with the idea of moving on from HSF at some point in the next year. On paper I’m on three months’ notice, but given the hearings lined up, it’s probably more like four months before I could leave without causing a mutiny. My partner has been making noises about “minimal flexibility on early release” and “team cohesion”, which doesn’t fill me with joy.

Complicating factor: I’d really like a short breather between roles. The last twelve months – Dubai, the ICSID European investor vs Latin American state, and the endless late nights at Exchange House – have properly wiped me out. In a perfect world I’d tack on two or three weeks after my notice ends just to be at home, do nursery drop‑offs, maybe even take Oliver to see my parents in Winchester mid‑week.

Is that remotely realistic in your view, or do people look at you like you’ve grown two heads if you say you don’t want to start immediately after leaving? I know you’ve seen a few people move between sets/firms – any war stories or tips on how honest one can be about needing a break would be appreciated.

Let me know when you’re next near the City – happy to buy you a pint and bore you with tales of Shoreditch nursery politics.

Best,

Dan

metadata.subject: Quick question about notice periods & sanity

artifacts.content:
[WhatsApp chat – Daniel & uni friend Priya]

Priya: You still thinking about that move you mentioned at Christmas?

Daniel: Sort of. I keep circling back to Volterra Fietta because of the public international law angle, but there are a few… complications.

Priya: Such as?

Daniel: For one thing, I’ve had some fairly tense run‑ins with one of their partners, Jonathan Blake, when we were opposite on an investment treaty case. Nothing dramatic, just frosty emails and a procedural scrap, but I doubt he’d send me a Christmas card.

Priya: Lol, that’s half the bar.

Daniel: True. Then there’s Jonathan Miller – remember my old flatmate from Bethnal Green in 2015–16? We ended up sleeping together after a messy night in Soho in May 2016 and tried to keep a casual thing going for a few months. It imploded that August when he started seeing someone in chambers and asked me not to tell anyone because he wasn’t out.

Priya: Oof.

Daniel: He joined VF as an associate in early 2022. We barely speak now, just the odd WhatsApp. In January last year he messaged from their Chancery Lane office saying I should apply and that he “owes me one” for keeping quiet about our past. Not exactly the cleanest professional connection.

Priya: That’s… layered.

Daniel: Add to that Dad’s experience – he did expert witness work in The Hague in 2019–20 for a Latin American state where VF were counsel. He felt one of their partners bullied him over his report and begged me never to mention his name if I end up there.

Priya: Wow. Maybe coffee first, decisions later.

Daniel: Exactly. Anyway, enough drama – how’s life at the UN circus?

Title: Networking / reputation – thoughts

- I keep worrying that I’ve been a bit too intense at networking things. When I think back to London International Disputes Week 2024, I probably came across as slightly pushy – cornering a couple of Volterra Fietta associates at drinks to talk about potential moves when they clearly just wanted to finish their wine and go home.

- Also that panel where Robert Volterra spoke about ESG and investment arbitration – I introduced myself afterwards, mentioned the ICSID work at HSF and asked about the police powers doctrine in climate regulation. It was fine, but I replay it in my head like I was some overeager LLM student again.

- Need to remember I do actually have proper experience now: LLB Bristol, LPC at BPP, the LLM in Public International Law at LSE (2015–16), admitted as a solicitor in Sept 2017, current practising certificate, years in the HSF international arbitration team.

- Connections:
  • Maria Fernández – now Counsel at VF, helped me through the failed dissertation draft / anxiety period at LSE when Crawford was ill.
  • Jonathan Miller – ex‑flatmate and complicated history; now an associate at VF near Chancery Lane.
  • That ICSID case where VF were co‑counsel for the state and their associate filed the witness statement with tracked changes showing cross‑examination strategy. I did late‑night research at Exchange House in April 2022 on whether tribunals ever sanction that kind of thing.

- Action point: at future conferences, focus on normal human conversation (family, travel, books) rather than launching straight into “so I’m thinking of moving firms…”.

[WhatsApp chat – Daniel & law school friend Hannah]

Hannah: You mentioned maybe looking at boutiques – any closer to deciding?

Daniel: Still dithering. Heard mixed things about Volterra Fietta, which is the obvious PIL choice.

Hannah: Mixed how?

Daniel: A friend from HSF, Sarah Patel, actually interviewed there last year. She ended up pulling out before final round – said the vibe was that the hours and expectations were somehow even harsher than at her magic circle shop. That’s literally my only “insider” view, second‑hand.

Hannah: Yikes.

Daniel: On the plus side, I do know a couple of people there. Maria from my LSE LLM days – she basically rescued my dissertation in 2016 when I’d failed the first draft and was a sleep‑deprived wreck. She’s now Counsel there and has always been encouraging about the public international law side of my practice.

Daniel: And then there’s the weird stuff: Dad’s expert witness stint in The Hague in 2019–20 where he felt one of their partners pushed him way too hard on his report, plus my own memories of that April 2022 ICSID case where their associate filed a witness statement with tracked changes showing their cross‑examination plan. I spent a grim night at Exchange House researching whether tribunals ever slap people on the wrist for that.

Hannah: Sounds like you’ve got plenty to talk about over coffee with them at least.

Daniel: True. Maybe I should just stop overthinking and go to one of their public lectures near Chancery Lane, see how it feels in the room.

metadata.location: Shoreditch Park (on a bench while Oliver plays)

artifacts.content:
Notes to self:

- Have You Previously Applied To Work For Or Been Employed By Volterra Fietta: false
